nodes.label: Configuration-Driven Module Loading
nodes.rationale: Hardcoded module registration makes process topology a code decision. Config-driven loading means the same binary can run as a full monolith, subset, or single-module isolated process with zero code changes at deploy time.

nodes.label: Two Module Tiers: IModule and ICoreModule
nodes.rationale: Outbox and BackgroundJobs are platform infrastructure that every writing instance needs (BaseDbContext atomically writes domain events to OutboxMessages). ICoreModule auto-loads these on every instance to prevent operator error of omitting required infrastructure.

nodes.label: IInterModuleRequestClient as Sole Sync Cross-Module Path
nodes.rationale: MassTransitInterModuleRequestClient always routes through RabbitMQ whether handler is in-process or remote. Call site is identical in both topologies — split deployment requires zero call-site rewrites.

nodes.label: IntegrationEvents for Async Cross-Module Communication
nodes.rationale: Reactive pattern — publisher does not know or care who consumes. Consumers are decoupled from producers at compile, runtime, and deployment. New modules can react to existing events with zero change to the publisher.

nodes.label: Transactional Outbox Pattern
nodes.rationale: Direct IPublishEndpoint calls create dual-write problem — DB write and publish are separate operations with crash window between them. BaseDbContext writes OutboxMessages atomically with aggregate state. OutboxProcessor uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to claim disjoint batches preventing duplicate publishing.

nodes.label: Each Module Owns Its Own DbContext
nodes.source_location: ### 6. Each module owns its own DbContext
nodes.rationale: Enforces data boundary ownership at the application layer. When a module splits to its own process, its DbContext connection string can point to a separate database via config change — no code migration required. Cross-module queries must go via IInterModuleRequestClient or event-driven denormalization, adding intentional friction.

nodes.label: DDD on Writes, VSA on Reads
nodes.source_location: ### 7. DDD on writes, VSA on reads
nodes.rationale: Writes need invariant protection via aggregates. Reads have no invariants to protect and benefit from direct AsNoTracking projection to DTOs for performance and clarity. Forcing reads through aggregates adds indirection with no benefit.

nodes.label: Compiler-Enforced Module Boundaries
nodes.rationale: Convention-based boundaries collapse under deadline pressure. Cross-module .csproj references fail the build immediately and locally — removing an entire class of architecture review comments. NetArchTest rules assert no cross-module Domain references.

nodes.label: Railway-Oriented Functional Pipeline
nodes.rationale: Imperative early-return chains require reading every branch to understand the happy path. Functional pipeline (BindAsync, TapAsync, MapAsync, CombineAsync) makes the happy path linear. Errors propagate automatically — failed BindAsync short-circuits the chain.

nodes.label: Develop as Monolith, Deploy as Microservices
nodes.rationale: Removes the multi-month 'extract later' infrastructure project entirely. Same codebase, same binary, one env var controls process topology. Communication contracts are remote-ready from the first line of code — no rewriting when splitting.

hyperedges.label: Transactional Outbox Atomicity: Aggregate + OutboxMessages in One DB Transaction
